title: Advanced Calculus
author: Kauffman, Robert M.
subject: calculus
abstract: This course guide has two objectives. First, we cover the major theorems of differential and integral calculus in two semesters, in such a fashion that the students in the class understand what they say and why it is true. Second, successful students will learn how to think logically as mathematicians do, and present the results. We apply these calculus theorems to the study of the elementary functions.

title: Advanced Calculus I
author: Parker, G. Edgar
subject: calculus
abstract: Topics include the logic of quantification, and a theory of sets and ordered pairs. This material is presented with a sequence of problems and theorems, so that students can be given the responsibility for both discovering and understanding why the mathematics covered in this course guide is correct.

title: Advanced Calculus II
author: Parker, G. Edgar
subject: calculus
abstract: This student-oriented course is a continuation of the study of the theory which supports elementary calculus. You will need a copy of the author's Advanced Calculus 1 course guide for reference.

title: Alexander-Wallace-Spanier Cohomology
author: Hildebrant, John A.
subject: topology
abstract: Topics include Abelian groups; cohomology groups; homomorphisms induced by continuous functions; reduced groups in dimension zero; fully normal spaces; inverse limits and the continuity theorem; strong excision theorems, extension, reduction; the map excision theorem; the Mayer-Vietoris theorem; codimension; marginal, peripheral, and inner points; the map addition theorem; acyclicity; and the Vietoris mapping theorem.

title: Better Mathematics Through Matrices
author: Tonne, Phil C.
subject: matrices
abstract: Topics include multiplication of matrices, solving matrix equations, inverses of matrices, commuting matrices, matrix polynomials, complex numbers, matrix similarity, and square roots and cube roots of matrices. The heart of this guide is a series of problems designed to engage your deductive skills, and stimulate your ability to find patterns and make generalizations. The goal is to improve your ability to reason; the vehicle is the study of matrices. Matrices are part of every branch of mathematics, and are a frequent tool in, for example, economics, sociology, and physics -- wherever data is organized.

title: Complex Inner Product Spaces
author: Mac Nerney, J. S.
subject: miscellaneous
abstract: It was the custom of Professor Mac Nerney, both at the University of North Carolina and the University of Houston, to offer his students, upon completion of his course on analytic functions, a further course. Here you will find the two themes of this additional course, each dear to the heart of Mac Nerney, Hellinger Integrals and Kernels in Functional Hilbert Spaces. The reader is invited to offer argument for the assertions that are true and counter-example to those that are not.

title: Elementary Mathematics from an Advanced Standpoint
author: Roach, Everett D.
subject: uncategorized
abstract: This book has been written for a two semester-hour course to precede calculus at the University of Dallas. I tried to make most of the material easy enough for the average students, but parts of it challenging enough for the brightest students. The topics discussed have been taken from the areas of mathematical logic, modern algebra, and trigonometry. There are two primary goals of this book. The first is to introduce the undergraduate to the process of mathematical reasoning early in his college studies. The second is to develop the skills needed to understand calculus. It is recommended that the students try to prove the theorems for which the proofs are given before reading the proofs.

title: First Graduate Course in Differential Equations
author: Neuberger, John W.
subject: differential equations
abstract: This material is indicative of John Neuberger's typical first year graduate course in differential equations. It extends H. S. Wall's undergraduate differential equations course. The similarity of much of this material with Courant and Hilbert's 'Methods of Mathematical Physics' is no coincidence. Hilbert, Hellinger, and Courant were prominent figures in Göttingen's great period, where Wall was a visitor for a year.

title: Foundations
author: Ochoa, James P.
subject: foundations
abstract: Topics include logic, sets, integer properties, induction, prime factorization, and principles of mathematical operations.

title: Foundations of Euclidean Geometry
author: Parker, G. Edgar
subject: geometry
abstract: The material in this course guide is presented with a sequence of problems and theorems, covering the logic of quantification, a theory of sets and ordered pairs, and the foundations of Euclidean geometry. Students will be responsible both for discovering and understanding why the mathematics covered in this course is correct.

title: Foundations of Mathematics
author: Clark, David M.
subject: foundations
university: State University of New York at New Paltz
abstract: This discovery guide is designed for a discovery-based foundations course for mathematics majors. While its primary goal is to develop a skill in problem-solving and theorem-proving, it simultaneously provides content knowledge of a range of topics that will be fundamental in subsequent mathematics courses. Topics include a formal view of set theory, propositional logic and Boolean expressions, functions, concepts of numbers, mathematical induction, natural deduction, and equivalence relations.

title: Foundations of Mathematics
author: Hildebrant, John A.
subject: foundations
abstract: Topics include sets and relations, the Peano axioms, order relations, the axiom of choice, finite and infinite sets, well-ordered sets, quotient sets and the integers, the real numbers, ordinal numbers, and cardinal numbers.

title: Geometry
author: Ochoa, James P.
subject: geometry
abstract: This guide provides a theorem sequence for an inquiry-based course in geometry, including such topics as points, lines, angles, betweenness, and congruence.

title: Graph Theory
author: Benedict, James M.
subject: graph theory
abstract: The discovery method, accredited to R. L. Moore, is the inspiration for the style of the presentation found within this text. The text is intended for undergraduates. It allows for a one-semester development of the most elementary yet universal concepts of Graph Theory. Topics include graph theoretic equality, degrees of vertices, subgraphs, complements of graphs, special subgraphs, the four-color theorem, planar graphs, the Petersen graph, and traceable graphs.

title: Jordan Curve Theorem
author: Mahavier, William S.
subject: topology
abstract: This theorem sequence on the Jordan Curve Theorem is probably best for students who have had at least an introduction to topology, and is certainly appropriate at the graduate level.

title: Languages and Machines
author: Clark, David M.
subject: uncategorized
university: State University of New York at New Paltz
abstract: This discovery-based course guide is primarily written for advanced undergraduate computer science and computer engineering students, who normally have no prior theorem-proving experience. In this course, students learn about some of the fundamental relationships between computing machines and the languages that they understand. Our goal in this course is to find ways to classify machines according to their complexity, and to understand the capabilities of machines in each complexity category. Topics include a review of tools from discrete mathematics, finite state automata and regular languages, and pushdown automata and context-free languages.

title: Linear Topology
author: Hagopian, Chuck
subject: topology
abstract: This course guide provides a sequence of definitions, theorems, axioms, problems, and questions, on topics of linear topology.

title: Matrix Theory
author: Whipple, Kenneth E.
subject: matrices
abstract: These notes were used in a university junior-level course taught by the author using the Moore method. Since matrix theory is so important to many areas of mathematics, it is especially important that students gain a strong intuition in this subject. This material gives students an introduction to the rigors of proving statements without having to deal with abstract concepts. Since the arithmetic of matrices resembles the arithmetic of numbers, many plausible but false conjectures occur, thus emphasizing the importance of careful treatment of the subject matter.

title: Number Theory
author: Whipple, Kenneth E.
subject: number theory
abstract: These notes cover topics usually found in an introductory course in number theory, including divisors, recursion, remainders, greatest common divisors, prime factors, congruence, the Chinese Remainder Theorem, Pythagorean triplets, using residue systems to solve modulo problems, quadratic residues and the Legendre function, reduced residue systems, integral powers, and the totient function.

title: Numerical Analysis: an Inquiry Based Approach
author: Bjork, Andrew-David
subject: uncategorized
abstract:
This set of notes was developed with the intent to help the students encounter various techniques from the field of numerical analysis. The primary goal is to have the students code computer programs that implement various classic algorithms from this discipline of mathematics. The author tried to keep the notes free from specific references to any particular software, though the course for which these notes were developed used Maple 17. I welcome all the feedback I can get on these notes so that I may improve them.
Note: This issue has an associated file. Please refer to Numerical Analysis: an Inquiry Based Approach (Student) for a Student version of this issue.

title: Probability Theory
author: Lane, Ralph E.
subject: discrete math
abstract: Topics include integrals, events, set functions, probability distributions, permutations, combinations, conditional probabilities, distribution functions, independence, expected values, and probability generating functions.

title: Theory of Equations
author: Creswell, Sam << Wall, H.S.
subject: foundations
abstract: These notes constitute an introduction to the number system, plane geometry, polynomials, and location of roots. This sophomore level course was developed and taught by Dr. Wall in an effort to introduce students to axiomatic mathematics at an early stage of their mathematical development.

title: Topology
author: Heath, Robert W.
subject: topology
abstract: This course guide provides a sequence of definitions, propositions, examples, and problems, on topics of topology.

title: Topology
author: Grace, Ed
subject: topology
abstract: This course guide provides a sequence of definitions, propositions, and questions, on topics of topology.

title: Topology
author: Anderson, Stuart
subject: topology
abstract: These are notes from a Texas-style topology course that James Ochoa took from Dr. Stuart Anderson at Texas A&M at Commerce.

title: Trigonometry
author: Reagh, Ed
subject: trigonometry
abstract: This course guide thoroughly covers all topics of trigonometry, at a level appropriate for high school students. Mr. Reagh worked as an actuary for Houston corporations, until retiring at age 45 to teach high school mathematics in a Houston suburb.

title: Vectors
author: Whipple, Kenneth E.
subject: geometry
abstract: Topics include a solid geometry review, directed line segments, parallels, angles and perpendiculars, vectors normal to a plane, cross products, and distances.
